skill.name: thinking-deeply
skill.description: Engages structured analysis to explore multiple perspectives and context dependencies before responding. Use when users ask confirmation-seeking questions, make leading statements, request binary choices, or when feeling inclined to quickly agree or disagree without thorough consideration.
skill.summary: Guides AI assistants to reframe questions, compare perspectives, and give context-aware recommendations.
skill.author: brunoasm

security_audit.summary: AI review found no confirmed security risks in the three scanned files. The static blocker findings are false positives from Markdown, JSON metadata, examples, and documentation links. No prompt injection, executable code, shell execution, cryptography, or data exfiltration behavior was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged weak cryptography, but the referenced lines contain skill descriptions, examples, or prior audit text. No cryptographic API, hashing code, encryption code, or executable script is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited files were reviewed and contain only JSON metadata, Markdown instructions, and decision-analysis examples. No code path invokes cryptographic primitives or weak algorithms.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged reconnaissance, but the referenced lines discuss considering perspectives, context, teams, and documentation examples. They do not request host enumeration, environment inspection, file discovery, or credential collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text is about analytical reasoning and comparing options. There is no instruction to inspect the runtime system, enumerate files, or gather secrets.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Shell Backtick Execution
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged Ruby or shell backticks in README.md. The backticks are Markdown formatting for a directory tree, a filename, and a YAML example, not executable Ruby or shell code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: README.md is documentation and contains no Ruby interpreter context or shell command execution. The static hits are ordinary Markdown fences and inline code spans.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Hardcoded Documentation URLs
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged hardcoded URLs in README.md. The URLs are passive documentation links to Claude support, Claude Skills documentation, and Anthropic research, not runtime network requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are Markdown references and are never fetched by skill code. No network client, request instruction, or data transmission behavior appears in the reviewed files.

content.value_statement: Quick answers can miss assumptions, trade-offs, and missing context. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to reframe questions and compare perspectives before recommending a path.
actual_capabilities: Identifies confirmation-seeking questions, leading statements, and binary choices.
Reframes narrow questions into broader decision problems.
Compares multiple perspectives before giving a recommendation.
Highlights context factors that can change the best answer.
Encourages balanced trade-off analysis instead of reflexive agreement.
Provides a structured response pattern for nuanced recommendations.
limitations: It does not run code, call APIs, or inspect external sources.
It cannot verify factual claims without another tool or provided evidence.
It may produce longer answers than a direct yes or no response.
It depends on the assistant recognizing when the prompt fits the trigger conditions.

use_cases.title: Compare Technical Options
use_cases.description: Evaluate languages, frameworks, libraries, or architectures with balanced trade-offs and context-specific recommendations.

use_cases.title: Challenge Product Assumptions
use_cases.description: Review strategy choices by surfacing hidden assumptions, alternatives, and decision factors before choosing a direction.

use_cases.title: Improve Analytical Reviews
use_cases.description: Explore complex questions from several viewpoints before writing recommendations, critiques, or research summaries.

prompt_templates.title: Beginner Comparison
prompt_templates.prompt: Should I choose option A or option B? Please compare the trade-offs before recommending one.
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when you need a balanced choice between two clear options.

prompt_templates.title: Assumption Check
prompt_templates.prompt: I think this is the obvious approach. What assumptions should we examine before deciding?
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when a decision feels too certain or one-sided.

prompt_templates.title: Context-Dependent Recommendation
prompt_templates.prompt: Given these goals, constraints, and risks, what recommendation changes under different conditions?
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when the best answer depends on missing or variable context.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Decision Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this decision from technical, operational, user, and long-term maintenance perspectives before giving a final recommendation.
prompt_templates.scenario: Use for high-impact decisions with several stakeholder concerns.

output_examples.input: React is better than Vue for this project, right?
output: The stronger question is which framework fits the project constraints.
React may fit complex applications, large teams, and hiring needs.
Vue may fit rapid delivery, smaller teams, and simpler onboarding.
The recommendation depends on team experience, timeline, project size, and maintenance expectations.

output_examples.input: Should we move this service to microservices?
output: The decision depends on whether the current architecture is creating real scaling or team coordination limits.
Microservices can improve independent delivery but add operational complexity.
A modular monolith may be better if the team is small or service boundaries are unclear.
The best next step is to identify the specific pain before changing architecture.

output_examples.input: Is TypeScript always better than JavaScript?
output: TypeScript is not universally better; it solves specific maintenance and tooling problems.
It is stronger for long-lived projects, shared codebases, and frequent refactoring.
JavaScript may be better for short scripts, prototypes, or teams avoiding build complexity.
The right choice depends on project lifespan, team skill, and tolerance for setup overhead.

best_practices: Provide goals, constraints, and decision criteria when asking for a recommendation.
Use it for choices with meaningful trade-offs, not simple factual questions.
Ask for missing context when the first answer depends on unstated assumptions.
anti_patterns: Do not use it to force long analysis for routine or already-settled tasks.
Do not treat every alternative as equally strong when evidence favors one option.
Do not skip the final recommendation after exploring trade-offs.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It guides the assistant to pause, reframe the question, compare perspectives, and give a context-aware recommendation.

faq.question: When should I use it?
faq.answer: Use it for confirmation questions, leading statements, binary choices, and decisions with important trade-offs.

faq.question: Does it execute code or access the network?
faq.answer: No. The reviewed skill is prompt guidance and documentation only.

faq.question: Will it always disagree with the user?
faq.answer: No. It is designed to examine assumptions and context, not to be contrarian.

faq.question: Can it help with software architecture choices?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples focus on frameworks, microservices, TypeScript, and similar technical decisions.

faq.question: What information improves the output?
faq.answer: Clear goals, constraints, risks, team context, timeline, and decision criteria improve the recommendation.
